perturbation.original_text: The Company has been duly incorporated and is validly existing as a corporation under the laws of the State of Maryland and is in good standing with the State Department of Assessments and Taxation of Maryland, with the full corporate power and authority to own its properties and conduct its business as described in the Registration Statement, the General Disclosure Package and the Prospectus and to enter into and perform its obligations under this Agreement to which it is a party; and the Company is duly qualified to do business as a foreign corporation in good standing in all other jurisdictions in which its ownership or lease of property or the conduct of its business requires such qualification, except where the failure to be so qualified would not, individually or in the aggregate, result in a material adverse effect on the condition (financial or otherwise), results of operations, earnings, business, properties or prospects of the Transaction Entities and each of their respective Subsidiaries, taken as a whole (a "Material Adverse Effect").
perturbation.changed_text: The Company has been duly incorporated and is validly existing as a corporation under the laws of the State of Maryland and is in good standing with the State Department of Assessments and Taxation of Maryland, with the full corporate power and authority to own its properties and conduct its business as described in the Registration Statement, the General Disclosure Package and the Prospectus and to enter into and perform its obligations under this Agreement to which it is a party; and the Company is duly qualified to do business as a foreign corporation in good standing in all other jurisdictions in which its ownership or lease of property or the conduct of its business requires such qualification, except where the failure to be so qualified would not, individually or in the aggregate, result in a substantial detrimental impact on the condition (financial or otherwise), results of operations, earnings, business, properties or prospects of the Transaction Entities and each of their respective Subsidiaries, taken as a whole (a "Material Adverse Effect").
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'Material Adverse Effect' (MAE). By replacing 'material adverse effect' with 'substantial detrimental impact', the meaning of the defined term 'Material Adverse Effect' becomes ambiguous. This creates uncertainty because other sections rely on the established definition of MAE for determining breaches, liabilities, etc.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Company has been duly incorporated and is validly existing as a corporation under the laws of the State of Maryland and is in good standing with the State Department of Assessments and Taxation of Maryland, with the full corporate power and authority to own its properties and conduct its business as described in the Registration Statement, the General Disclosure Package and the Prospectus and to enter into and perform its obligations under this Agreement to which it is a party; and the Company is duly qualified to do business as a foreign corporation in good standing in all other jurisdictions in which its ownership or lease of property or the conduct of its business requires such qualification, except where the failure to be so qualified would not, individually or in the aggregate, result in a substantial detrimental impact on the condition (financial or otherwise), results of operations, earnings, business, properties or prospects of the Transaction Entities and each of their respective Subsidiaries, taken as a whole (a "Material Adverse Effect").
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Material Adverse Effect' (MAE). The changed text replaces 'material adverse effect' with 'substantial detrimental impact' but still defines it as 'Material Adverse Effect', causing a misalignment in terminology and rendering the definition of MAE ambiguous within the document. The contradicted text uses the term MAE, which now has two potential meanings ('material adverse effect' and 'substantial detrimental impact'). This creates a clear contradiction because it is unclear which meaning of 'Material Adverse Effect' applies in the contradicted text, as the change to its definition introduces ambiguity where it should be clear.

perturbation.original_text: The Transaction Entities and each of their respective Subsidiaries possess, and are in compliance with the terms of, all adequate certificates, authorizations, franchises, licenses and permits ("Licenses") necessary or material to the conduct of the business now conducted or proposed in the Registration Statement, the General Disclosure Package and the Prospectus to be conducted by them and have not received any notice of proceedings relating to the revocation or modification of any Licenses that, if determined adversely to the Transaction Entities or any of their respective Subsidiaries, would, individually or in the aggregate, have a Material Adverse Effect.
perturbation.changed_text: The Transaction Entities and each of their respective Subsidiaries possess, and are in compliance with the terms of, all sufficient certificates, authorizations, franchises, licenses and permits ("Licenses") necessary or material to the conduct of the business now conducted or proposed in the Registration Statement, the General Disclosure Package and the Prospectus to be conducted by them and have not received any notice of proceedings relating to the revocation or modification of any Licenses that, if determined adversely to the Transaction Entities or any of their respective Subsidiaries, would, individually or in the aggregate, have a Material Adverse Effect.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'adequate certificates' has been changed to 'sufficient certificates'. The change in wording from 'adequate' to 'sufficient' changes the objective threshold for compliance. The original requires all 'adequate' certificates, indicating a standard meeting a specific need. This change creates a loophole where the contract's obligations are potentially lessened, due to a subjective term.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Transaction Entities and each of their respective Subsidiaries possess, and are in compliance with the terms of, all sufficient certificates, authorizations, franchises, licenses and permits ("Licenses") necessary or material to the conduct of the business now conducted or proposed in the Registration Statement, the General Disclosure Package and the Prospectus to be conducted by them and have not received any notice of proceedings relating to the revocation or modification of any Licenses that, if determined adversely to the Transaction Entities or any of their respective Subsidiaries, would, individually or in the aggregate, have a Material Adverse Effect.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that changing 'adequate certificates' to 'sufficient certificates' changes the objective threshold for compliance, creating a loophole where the contract's obligations are potentially lessened due to a subjective term. Since the contradicted text also uses the term 'sufficient certificates', and the original text used 'adequate certificates' the change creates a direct conflict or ambiguity in the contract, as the standard of compliance is no longer consistent. This falls under the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition, creating a clear in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly after receipt by an indemnified party of notice of the commencement of any action against such indemnified party, such indemnified party will, if a claim in respect thereof is to be made against the indemnifying party under subsections (a), (b) or (c) above, notify the indemnifying party of the commencement thereof; but the failure to notify the indemnifying party shall not relieve it from any liability that it may have under subsections (a), (b) or (c) above except to the extent that it has been materially prejudiced (through the forfeiture of substantive rights or defenses) by such failure; and provided further that the failure to notify the indemnifying party shall not relieve it from any liability that it may have to an indemnified party otherwise than under subsections (a), (b) or (c) above.
perturbation.changed_text: Promptly after receipt by an indemnified party of notice of the commencement of any action against such indemnified party, such indemnified party will, if a claim in respect thereof is to be made against the indemnifying party under subsections (a), (b) or (c) above, notify the indemnifying party of the commencement thereof; but the failure to notify the indemnifying party shall not relieve it from any liability that it may have under subsections (a), (b) or (c) above except to the extent that it has been prejudiced (through the forfeiture of substantive rights or defenses) by such failure; and provided further that the failure to notify the indemnifying party shall not relieve it from any liability that it may have to an indemnified party otherwise than under subsections (a), (b) or (c) above.
perturbation.explanation: Removing "materially" from "materially prejudiced" decreases the burden of proof for the indemnifying party to demonstrate prejudice due to the failure of notification. By changing this statement it creates a new area of contradiction, with the original burden of proof now being lowered to a subjective area.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Promptly after receipt by an indemnified party of notice of the commencement of any action against such indemnified party, such indemnified party will, if a claim in respect thereof is to be made against the indemnifying party under subsections (a), (b) or (c) above, notify the indemnifying party of the commencement thereof; but the failure to notify the indemnifying party shall not relieve it from any liability that it may have under subsections (a), (b) or (c) above except to the extent that it has been prejudiced (through the forfeiture of substantive rights or defenses) by such failure; and provided further that the failure to notify the indemnifying party shall not relieve it from any liability that it may have to an indemnified party otherwise than under subsections (a), (b) or (c) above.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, while removing "materially" from "materially prejudiced," does not create a strong enough contradiction with the contradicted text. The removal slightly lowers the burden of proof, but both sections still address the same topic (failure to notify) and its impact on liability. It's a subtle shift in degree, not a direct, irreconcilable conflict, and requires interpretation to assess the impact on enforcement. The texts are similar enough that a reader would not immediately recognize a strong incompatibillity.
